story:
Matt Sanders, a meticulous thief posing as a priest on a luxury cruise, opens yet another jewelry box and finds it empty. Twice now his “perfect” marks—elderly, wealthy, and predictable—have yielded nothing. He doesn’t believe in luck and knows this isn’t it. It feels like competition.

Brooding in the Deep Blue Lounge over a bourbon—Maker’s Mark, on the rocks—he tugs at his Roman collar and tries to think. The bartender mentions a note from Dave, the day-shift guy: “the good stuff for the priest.” Matt lifts his glass, determined to drink alone. Then a familiar sultry voice slides in beside him. Sabrina. Stunning, dangerous, and the last person he expected to see—especially out of cuffs. Five years earlier, during the botched job on the Red Haired Maiden, he ran when a widow unexpectedly came back with her burly nephew. She didn’t. She did time. Now she’s out on good behavior.

Their banter is easy, sharp. She teases him about a “Saul” conversion; he tries to apologize; she waves it off. And then he sees it: of course she’s the one beating him to his targets. Who else knows his habits, his marks, his methods? Excitement replaces irritation. He pitches a reunion: one more job, one night left, “two heads are better than one.” She smiles and warns that a friendly priest flirting might get reported to the bishop.

They choose cabin 302, home of a New York publishing matron who always lingers over dinner and then keeps Jim Beam company in the lounge. Sabrina takes the lookout spot. Matt pushes away doubts about old grudges and goes to work. From a hollowed-out breviary he selects the right pick. A clean click. He slips inside—and is blinded by a flashlight that isn’t his.

It’s Dave. The day-shift bartender. Caught mid-heist.

Before Matt can process it, security floods the room. Hands yanked back. Steel on wrists. Dave is cuffed too. Sabrina appears in the doorway, a ship security badge glinting on a silver chain. Calm. Professional. Very much on the “other side.”

She didn’t lie, she says; he assumed. Prison gave her a conversion of sorts—one he, of all people in a collar, should understand. While he’d been casing marks, she’d seen the pattern, remembered him, and set the trap. Whether Dave had been the one emptying those earlier boxes or simply the latest thief to prowl, Sabrina’s timing snags them both. Matt protests that she wants Dave, not him; he didn’t lift a cent. He was only in the room. She tugs his cuffs, leans close, and reminds him of his favorite line: two heads are always better than one.

The sting lands clean. The priest’s collar was an old cover, the bourbon a brief comfort, the reunion an illusion. Sabrina, once his partner and equal, has become his mirror image: still smart, still cool, now legit—using the same instincts to protect instead of steal. Matt, who built a career on planning and control, is outmaneuvered not by luck, but by someone who knows him too well and plays the long game better.
